div.head: The Chargé in the United Kingdom (Gallman) to the Secretary of State
div.p: 9807. Ronald told us this evening that a cable had been sent this afternoon to the British Embassy in Washington instructing it to urge upon the Department the necessity for an early reply to the Soviet note regarding the Polish representation at the EITO Conference.

We were shown an account of a conversation between Mr. EdenAnthony Eden, British Secretary of State for Foreign Affairs. and the Soviet Ambassador on November 7 during which Mr. Eden stated that the British Government could in no circumstances assent to the representation of the Lublin Committee at the expense of the Polish Government in London. Ronald also told us that Richard LawRichard K. Law, British Minister of State. had, on October 31, made a similar statement to Mr. GousevFedor Tarasovitch Gousev, Soviet Ambassador in the United Kingdom. in which he expressed surprise that the Soviet Government had taken such a step while the Polish question was still being considered on the basis of the talks held in Moscow by the Prime Minister and Mr. Eden.

The Soviet Ambassador has requested a written statement of the British Government viewpoint and this the Foreign Office states it cannot give until it knows how the Department intends to answer the Soviet note.
